chemicals.concentration_notes: The plant contains roughly 38 different cardiac glycosides, making it powerfully toxic despite its dainty appearance46. These toxins are heavily concentrated throughout all parts of the plant (including the bulb, roots, rhizomes, stems, leaves, and flowers) and will even leach into the water of a vase holding the cut flowers
chemicals.description: in cats: These toxic sugars directly target the heart by inhibiting the Na + /K + -ATPase pump23. This inhibition causes an increase in intracellular sodium, which subsequently triggers an increase in intracellular calcium3. This cellular disruption significantly alters electrical conduction and how the cardiac muscle contracts, leading to profound cardiovascular depression, severe cardiac arrhyth
chemicals.name: Cardiac glycosides, specifically identified as convallarin, convallamarin, and convallatoxin

chemicals.concentration_notes: Like the cardiac glycosides, these irritant saponins are distributed throughout the entire plant17. (Note: While Lily of the Valley is highly poisonous and can disrupt normal heart activity and the gastrointestinal tract, the sources emphasize that it does not cause the acute, fatal kidney failure associated with "true lilies" of the Lilium and Hemerocallis families46.)
chemicals.description: in cats: Working alongside the cardiac glycosides, saponins act as severe gastrointestinal irritants1.... When a cat ingests the plant, these compounds trigger significant gastrointestinal upset, causing severe abdominal pain, vomiting, and diarrhea (which may sometimes contain blood)1.... 4. Notes on
chemicals.name: Irritant saponins

description: Lily of the Valley (Convallaria majalis) is one of the most dangerous plants for cats. Despite its delicate appearance, every part — flowers, leaves, stems, berries, and even the water in a vase — contains potent cardiac glycosides including convallatoxin, convalloside, and over 30 related compounds. These affect the heart in a manner similar to Foxglove, and even tiny amounts can be lethal.
name: Lily of the Valley
safetyNotes: Toxic compounds: convallatoxin, convalloside, and 30+ related cardiac glycosides; highest in flowers and berries
Mechanism: cardiac glycosides inhibit Na+/K+-ATPase in heart muscle, disrupting electrical conduction and causing severe arrhythmias
Symptoms: vomiting, drooling, bradycardia (dangerously slow heart rate), hypotension, cardiac arrhythmias, weakness, collapse; can be fatal
All plant parts are hazardous — even the water in a flower vase can cause toxicity
Any ingestion is a veterinary emergency; do not wait for symptoms to develop

symptoms.name: Vomiting, diarrhea (which may contain blood), abdominal pain/discomfort, and trembling
symptoms.notes: These symptoms are primarily triggered by the irritant saponins found throughout the plant12. The severe irritation of the gastrointestinal tract often precedes more dangerous systemic cardiovascular issues1. 2. Cardiovascular Depression and Arrhythmias
symptoms.onset: Variable latent period depending on the dose;

symptoms.name: Bradycardia (a sudden, significant decrease in heart rate) and severe cardiac arrhythmias (such as Atrioventricular or AV block)
symptoms.notes: This is the most dangerous consequence of Lily of the Valley toxicity and is caused by the plant's cardiac glycosides2.... These toxins alter how the cardiac muscle contracts and disrupt electrical conduction57. In acute cases, this can also lead to hyperkalemia (dangerously high potassium levels in
symptoms.onset: Variable latent period depending on the dose;

symptoms.name: Seizures and tremors
symptoms.notes: Seizures and trembling are secondary clinical signs that manifest as the toxins profoundly depress the cardiovascular system and disrupt cellular functions15. (Note: While these symptoms are highly dangerous and potentially fatal, veterinary sources emphasize that Lily of the Valley does not cause t
symptoms.onset: Variable; usually develops as the cardiovascular depression worsens

treatments.description: If the cat is asymptomatic and the ingestion is recent, the veterinarian will induce vomiting (emesis) or pump the stomach (gastric lavage) to remove the plant material. This is followed by administering a dose of activated charcoal with a cathartic to bind any remaining toxins in the gut.
treatments.name: Gastrointestinal Decontamination
treatments.notes: Because these specific toxins can recirculate through the liver and intestines (enterohepatic recirculation), the vet will typically administer repeated doses of activated charcoal (without a cathartic) every 6 to 8 hours for a total of 3 doses12. 2. Antidote Administration (Digoxin-specific Antibod

treatments.description: The veterinarian administers a specific antidote that is structurally designed to reverse digoxin toxicity, which cross-reacts and works effectively against the plant-based cardiac glycosides found in Lily of the Valley2.
treatments.name: Administration of Digoxin-specific antibody fragments (Fab)
treatments.notes: While highly effective, this antidote may not always be readily available. If access to the antidote is unavailable, the veterinarian will rely heavily on the pharmacological treatments listed below to manage the symptoms2. 3. Pharmacological Cardiac Control (Antiarrhythmics)

treatments.description: The vet will use specific medications to stabilize the cat's heart. Atropine or glycopyrrolate is administered to correct bradycardia (a dangerously slow heart rate), while antiarrhythmic drugs like lidocaine or phenytoin are used to manage other heart rhythm abnormalities and conduction defects12.
treatments.name: Heart Rate and Arrhythmia Correction (Atropine, Lidocaine, Phenytoin)
treatments.notes: During this treatment, the cat will require continuous, intensive electrocardiographic (ECG) monitoring to track the heart's electrical activity and adjust medications as needed1. 4. IV Fluid Therapy and Severe Electrolyte Management

treatments.description: The cat is given IV fluids to provide hemodynamic support and manage dehydration from vomiting or diarrhea. Concurrently, the vet will monitor serum potassium levels, as acute poisoning can cause hyperkalemia (dangerously high potassium levels in the blood)12.
treatments.name: Intravenous (IV) Fluid Therapy and Hyperkalemia Treatment
treatments.notes: Mild hyperkalemia may be resolved with IV fluids alone. However, if the hyperkalemia becomes severe (greater than 7.5–9 mEq/L), the veterinarian will need to treat it aggressively using sodium bicarbonate or a combination of dextrose and insulin therapy
